bash:
BASH(1)                    General Commands Manual          BASH(1)

NAME
       bash - GNU Bourne-Again SHell

SYNOPSIS
       bash [options] [command_string | file]

DESCRIPTION
       bash is the default interactive shell in Rocky Linux. It reads commands
       from standard input or a script, supports variables, aliases, history,
       pipes, and shell builtins, and is commonly used for day-to-day admin
       work and automation.

OPTIONS
       -c string
              Execute commands from string.

       -l
              Start as login shell.

       -i
              Force interactive mode.

       -x
              Print commands and their arguments as they execute.

       -e
              Exit immediately if a command exits non-zero.

EXAMPLES
       bash
              Start an interactive shell.

       bash -c "echo hello"
              Execute one command string.

       bash script.sh
              Execute script.sh.

       bash -x script.sh
              Execute with xtrace output.

       bash -c "set -e; false; echo never"
              Demonstrate errexit behavior.

POCKETTERM NOTE
       PocketTerm simulates bash semantics in a browser runtime, so process
       control and host-level syscalls are intentionally sandboxed.

SEE ALSO
       sh(1), man(1), export(1), source(1)
ls:
LS(1)                      User Commands                    LS(1)

NAME
       ls - list directory contents

SYNOPSIS
       ls [OPTION]... [FILE]...

DESCRIPTION
       ls lists file and directory names. With flags it can show hidden files,
       permissions, owners, sizes, and timestamps. It is the first command most
       users run to inspect filesystem state.

OPTIONS
       -a, --all
              Include entries beginning with .

       -l
              Use long listing format.

       -h, --human-readable
              Show sizes in human-readable units with -l.

       -t
              Sort by modification time, newest first.

       -r, --reverse
              Reverse sort order.

EXAMPLES
       ls
              List current directory.

       ls -la
              List all entries in long format.

       ls /etc
              List the /etc directory.

       ls -lh /var/log
              Long format with human-readable sizes.

       ls -lt
              Sort entries by time, newest first.

POCKETTERM NOTE
       In terminal mode ls uses compact columns; in pipeline mode output is
       line-oriented for realistic downstream filtering behavior.

SEE ALSO
       cd(1), pwd(1), stat(1)
grep:
GREP(1)                    User Commands                   GREP(1)

NAME
       grep - print lines matching a pattern

SYNOPSIS
       grep [OPTIONS] PATTERN [FILE...]

DESCRIPTION
       grep searches input for lines that match PATTERN and prints matching
       lines. Input may come from files or standard input through a pipeline.
       grep is fundamental for log triage, config inspection, and scripting.

OPTIONS
       -i
              Ignore case distinctions.

       -n
              Prefix each output line with line number.

       -v
              Select non-matching lines.

       -c
              Print only a count of matching lines.

       -r
              Recursively search directories.

EXAMPLES
       grep error /var/log/messages
              Find lines containing "error".

       grep -i failed auth.log
              Case-insensitive search for "failed".

       grep -n nameserver /etc/resolv.conf
              Show line numbers with matches.

       dnf list available | grep vim
              Filter command output in a pipeline.

       grep -c sshd /var/log/secure
              Print the number of matching lines.

POCKETTERM NOTE
       grep participates in the simulator line model used by wc -l so final
       unterminated text chunks still count consistently in interactive flows.

SEE ALSO
       awk(1), sed(1), less(1)
lynx:
LYNX(1)                     User Commands                    LYNX(1)

NAME
       lynx - text-mode web browser (simulation)

SYNOPSIS
       lynx [-dump] URL

DESCRIPTION
       lynx fetches a web page and renders a plaintext view suitable for
       terminal workflows. In PocketTerm, this command performs a browser-safe
       HTTP fetch and converts HTML to readable text.

OPTIONS
       -dump    Print text-only output for the target page.

EXAMPLES
       lynx https://example.com
       lynx -dump example.com

POCKETTERM NOTE
       lynx uses browser-backed fetch and may be affected by CORS policy,
       offline state, browser privacy settings, or blocked remote endpoints.

SEE ALSO
       curl(1), wget(1)
